annotations.text: Several pathological categories, including traumatic, metabolic, congenital, neoplastic, and inflammatory lesions, deserve mention in the differential diagnosis of spinal-cord dysfunction associated with cervical vertebral lesions.
A fracture with a hematoma can cause a mass compressing the cord, but one would expect to see bone fragments, which were not shown in this case.
The most common lesions of the spine associated with neurologic compromise are tumors and infection.
The first question raised in the differential diagnosis of post-traumatic pain is whether the trauma itself explains the symptoms and findings.
The demonstration of erosion of two adjacent vertebral-body end plates with narrowing of the intervening disk space has been considered the radiographic hallmark of infection for over half a century.
the pattern of involvement of the disk space and adjoining vertebral end plates and the elevated sedimentation rate.
I have no way of being sure that the paronychia had any causal relation to the presumptive spinal infection.
Factors favoring a pyogenic infection include a short history, as in this case, and the early appearance of neurologic symptoms.

there is a case report of a health care worker who had a paronychia as a result of a direct inoculation of the finger with tubercle bacilli, and without further details about this patient's occupational exposures as a surgical assistant, tuberculosis remains a theoretical if remote possibility.
